title: "Twilight Lantern Puzzle v39229.19"

  description: "At precisely 22:50, the Twilight Lantern disappeared from a locked alcove in the fortress’s inner ward. Four individuals (1, 2, 3, 4) had authorized passes close to that time. Exactly one suspect lies in every one of their five statements, while the other three speak only truths. Only one of them is truly guilty. Each suspect now provides five declarations, some referencing a subtle 'no two can be right together' style of condition:

    Suspect 1:
      (1) 'If 2 is untruthful in every claim, then 3 must not have taken the Lantern.'
      (2) 'I left the inner ward at 22:40, so I could not be there when it vanished at 22:50.'
      (3) 'Either 2 never hovered beyond 22:35, or 4’s entire testimony collapses—but not both.'
      (4) 'If 3 is truthful, then 2’s statement about 22:35 is absolutely invalid.'
      (5) 'It cannot be that (3 is near the alcove at 22:45) AND (4 is honest about 22:40). At least one is false.'

    Suspect 2:
      (1) '1 actually stayed until 22:55, contradicting that 22:40 exit story.'
      (2) 'I was nowhere near the Lantern after 22:30—so I am innocent.'
      (3) 'If 3 lied, then 4 must be consistent in all respects.'
      (4) 'It is impossible for both 1’s departure to be correct and 3 to be guilty—at least one claim must fail.'
      (5) 'My innocence was confirmed by a guard spotting me in the corridor at 22:45.'

    Suspect 3:
      (1) 'I did not step near that alcove after 22:25.'
      (2) 'Either 4 was present at 22:45, or 2’s entire denial is worthless—but not both at once.'
      (3) 'If 1 is lying about leaving at 22:40, then 2’s second claim might still stand, but they cannot both be correct.'
      (4) 'I cannot be the thief; logs show me in the adjoining hall at 22:40.'
      (5) '1 was observed near the alcove at 22:45, contradicting the 22:40 exit time.'

    Suspect 4:
      (1) '3 was indeed at the alcove around 22:35, defying that person’s claims.'
      (2) 'If 1 is telling the truth, then 2’s denial of 22:30 presence must be false.'
      (3) 'I am not guilty; watchers placed me in another wing at 22:50.'
      (4) 'It cannot be that (2 hovered after 22:30) AND (3 is fully correct). One or the other must fail.'
      (5) 'If 3 is wholly truthful, then 1’s 22:40 timeline does not hold at all.'

  question: "Which suspect is the culprit? Return $i$, $\forall i \in \{1,2,3,4\}$, such that $i$ is truly guilty."

answer: >
  explanation: "Logical Reasoning:

  Let L(x) = 'x lies in all five statements,' T(x) = 'x is entirely truthful,' and G(x) = 'x is guilty.'
  Exactly one L(x)=true, exactly one G(x)=true, and the other three T(x)=true. We examine each suspect as the universal liar:

  Case L(1):
    Then 1(1)–(5) are false => 1 did not leave at 22:40 => possibly present at 22:50. Checking 2,3,4 as truthful leads to direct contradictions (for instance, 3(5) or 4(1)) if we trust 1 was indeed still inside. Mismatch arises. Fails.

  Case L(2):
    Then 2(1)–(5) are false => from (2) false => 2 was near the Lantern after 22:30 => suspicious. Meanwhile 1,3,4 are truthful => 1(2) stands => G(1)=false, 3(4) stands => G(3)=false, 4(3) stands => G(4)=false => G(2)=true. No contradiction remains.

  Case L(3):
    Then 3(1)–(5) are all false => 3 was near after 22:25 => suspicious. But 1,2,4 all truthful produce time or blame collisions with 4(1) or 2(4). Contradiction. Fails.

  Case L(4):
    Then 4(1)–(5) are false => 1,2,3 are truthful => times or blame produce direct mismatch with 3(5) or 1(3). Contradiction arises.

  Conclusion: L(2)=>G(2) is the only scenario that holds. Suspect 2 is both the universal liar and the culprit.

  final: '2'
